prompt.text: In the context of biology and health, which of the following best defines a 'pathogen'?

options: A beneficial bacterium that helps the body digest complex carbohydrates.
An infectious agent, such as a virus, bacterium, or fungus, that causes disease in its host.
A specialized white blood cell that identifies and destroys cancerous tissue.
A type of environmental toxin, like lead or mercury, that damages the nervous system.
questions.answer: An infectious agent, such as a virus, bacterium, or fungus, that causes disease in its host.

prompt.text: Which of the following is NOT classified as a pathogen?

options: A fungus that causes athlete's foot
A vector, such as a mosquito, that carries a disease
A virus that causes the common cold
A bacterium that causes strep throat
questions.answer: A vector, such as a mosquito, that carries a disease

prompt.text: What are the two primary types of immunity that work together to protect the vertebrate body?

options: Active immunity and Passive immunity
Innate immunity and Adaptive immunity
Bacterial immunity and Viral immunity
Internal immunity and External immunity
questions.answer: Innate immunity and Adaptive immunity

prompt.text: A broad, rapid response that includes physical barriers like skin and internal defenses like inflammation.

categories: Innate Immunity
Adaptive Immunity
questions.answer: Innate Immunity

prompt.text: A highly specific response involving B cells and T cells that 'remembers' previous infections.

categories: Innate Immunity
Adaptive Immunity
questions.answer: Adaptive Immunity

prompt.text: Phagocytic cells, such as macrophages, that consume any foreign particles they encounter.

categories: Innate Immunity
Adaptive Immunity
questions.answer: Innate Immunity

prompt.text: The production of customized antibodies designed to neutralize a specific strain of virus.

categories: Innate Immunity
Adaptive Immunity
questions.answer: Adaptive Immunity

prompt.text: What is the primary process by which specialized immune cells, such as macrophages and neutrophils, ingest and destroy invading pathogens?

options: Pinocytosis
Exocytosis
Phagocytosis
Apoptosis
questions.answer: Phagocytosis

prompt.text: Which of the following are considered the body's primary 'barrier defenses' within the innate immune system?

options: B cells, T cells, and memory cells
Skin, mucous membranes, and secretions like lysozyme
Inflammation, fever, and the release of histamine
Antibodies, antigens, and cytotoxic proteins
questions.answer: Skin, mucous membranes, and secretions like lysozyme

prompt.text: What is the primary function of Toll-like receptors (TLRs) in the innate immune system?

options: To produce customized antibodies that target specific viral proteins.
To recognize general molecular patterns common to groups of pathogens and trigger an immune response.
To act as a physical barrier that prevents bacteria from entering the bloodstream.
To transport oxygen to infected tissues to speed up the healing process.
questions.answer: To recognize general molecular patterns common to groups of pathogens and trigger an immune response.

prompt.text: Which two types of cells are the primary phagocytic agents that circulate in the body to ingest and destroy pathogens?

options: B cells and T cells
Neutrophils and Macrophages
Erythrocytes and Platelets
Basophils and Eosinophils
questions.answer: Neutrophils and Macrophages

prompt.text: What is the primary role of eosinophils within the innate immune system?

options: To produce large quantities of antibodies during a secondary infection.
To discharge destructive enzymes that combat large, multicellular parasites like worms.
To act as the primary structural component of the skin's barrier defense.
To filter lymph fluid within the specialized nodes of the neck and groin.
questions.answer: To discharge destructive enzymes that combat large, multicellular parasites like worms.

prompt.text: How do dendritic cells contribute to the overall immune response?

options: They remain in the bloodstream to transport oxygen to site of injury.
They act as 'scouts' that ingest pathogens and then migrate to lymph nodes to activate adaptive immunity.
They form the physical mucus layer that traps dust and pollen in the trachea.
They are the primary cells responsible for the production of histamine during an allergic reaction.
questions.answer: They act as 'scouts' that ingest pathogens and then migrate to lymph nodes to activate adaptive immunity.

prompt.text: What is the primary function of Natural Killer (NK) cells in the innate immune system?

options: They ingest bacteria through phagocytosis and digest them in vacuoles.
They identify and release chemicals to kill infected or cancerous body cells.
They produce memory cells that provide long-term immunity against viruses.
They create a physical barrier on the skin to prevent pathogen entry.
questions.answer: They identify and release chemicals to kill infected or cancerous body cells.

prompt.text: What role do mast cells play in the body's response to an injury or infection?

options: They circulate in the blood to transport carbon dioxide away from the wound.
They release histamine, which triggers blood vessels to dilate and become more permeable.
They produce customized antibodies to neutralize specific bacterial toxins.
They transform into macrophages to eat dead tissue at the site of the injury.
questions.answer: They release histamine, which triggers blood vessels to dilate and become more permeable.

prompt.text: How does the inflammatory response facilitate the movement of immune cells to the site of an infection?

options: By constricting blood vessels to trap pathogens in a localized area until they starve.
By triggering blood vessel dilation and increased permeability, which amplifies chemical signals and allows immune cells to easily exit the bloodstream.
By thickening the blood to prevent signaling molecules from reaching the brain and causing pain.
By reducing blood flow to the area to prevent the amplification of inflammatory markers.
questions.answer: By triggering blood vessel dilation and increased permeability, which amplifies chemical signals and allows immune cells to easily exit the bloodstream.

prompt.text: What is the composition and primary significance of 'pus' at the site of an inflammatory response?

options: It is a collection of beneficial vitamins used to rebuild the skin's surface layer.
It is a fluid rich in white blood cells, dead pathogens, and cell debris, signaling that the immune system is actively fighting an infection.
It is a specialized mucus used to transport oxygen directly to damaged muscle tissue.
It is a liquid form of histamine that prevents blood from clotting too quickly at the wound site.
questions.answer: It is a fluid rich in white blood cells, dead pathogens, and cell debris, signaling that the immune system is actively fighting an infection.

prompt.text: What eventually happens to the pus that accumulates at the site of a resolving infection?

options: It is permanently hardened into a calcium deposit to strengthen the surrounding tissue.
It is gradually absorbed by the body's lymphatic system and digested by macrophages.
It is converted into red blood cells to replenish the volume lost during the inflammatory phase.
It evaporates through the skin's pores as part of the body's cooling mechanism.
questions.answer: It is gradually absorbed by the body's lymphatic system and digested by macrophages.

prompt.text: What role do the lymph nodes play in the 'cleanup' phase of an infection?

options: They act as the primary site for the production of red blood cells to replace those lost in pus.
They filter the lymphatic fluid, allowing specialized immune cells to destroy the remaining pathogens and cellular debris.
They secrete a high-pressure fluid that pushes pus out through the skin's pores.
They store the pus indefinitely to prevent it from ever reaching the heart.
questions.answer: They filter the lymphatic fluid, allowing specialized immune cells to destroy the remaining pathogens and cellular debris.

prompt.text: What is the primary role of interferons in the body's innate defense against viruses?

options: They act as digestive enzymes that dissolve the outer shell of any bacteria they touch.
They are proteins secreted by virus-infected cells that signal neighboring uninfected cells to produce substances that inhibit viral replication.
They are structural proteins that thicken the cell membrane to physically block viruses from entering.
They are chemical signals that trigger the brain to increase body temperature to kill the virus through heat.
questions.answer: They are proteins secreted by virus-infected cells that signal neighboring uninfected cells to produce substances that inhibit viral replication.

prompt.text: What is the primary role of the complement system within the immune response?

options: To produce oxygen for muscle cells during periods of high inflammation.
A group of proteins that circulate in an inactive state and can be activated to lyse (burst) pathogens or mark them for destruction.
To act as the main structural component of the bone marrow where blood cells are born.
To permanently store viral DNA so the body can use it to build new cell membranes.
questions.answer: A group of proteins that circulate in an inactive state and can be activated to lyse (burst) pathogens or mark them for destruction.

prompt.text: Which of the following best describes the fundamental difference in how the innate and adaptive immune systems respond to a pathogen?

options: Innate immunity provides a rapid, non-specific response to broad groups of pathogens, while adaptive immunity provides a slower, highly specific response that develops 'memory'.
Innate immunity uses antibodies to target specific viruses, while adaptive immunity uses physical barriers like skin to block all bacteria.
Innate immunity is only found in invertebrates, while adaptive immunity is the only system found in vertebrates like humans.
Innate immunity is activated only after the adaptive immune system fails to clear an infection after several weeks.
questions.answer: Innate immunity provides a rapid, non-specific response to broad groups of pathogens, while adaptive immunity provides a slower, highly specific response that develops 'memory'.

prompt.text: How do vaccines utilize the adaptive immune system's 'memory' to protect the body against future infections?

options: By providing a high dose of general antibodies that circulate in the bloodstream for the rest of a person's life.
By introducing a harmless version of a pathogen to trigger a primary immune response and the creation of long-lasting memory cells.
By temporarily strengthening the skin's physical barrier to prevent any pathogens from entering the body.
By increasing the speed of the innate inflammatory response to ensure all pathogens are destroyed within minutes.
questions.answer: By introducing a harmless version of a pathogen to trigger a primary immune response and the creation of long-lasting memory cells.

prompt.text: In the context of the adaptive immune system, what is an 'antigen'?

options: A specialized protein produced by B cells to neutralize viruses in the blood.
Any substance or part of a pathogen that is recognized by immune cells and triggers a specific immune response.
A chemical signal released by damaged tissues to initiate the inflammatory response.
A protective coating found on the surface of helpful gut bacteria to prevent them from being digested.
questions.answer: Any substance or part of a pathogen that is recognized by immune cells and triggers a specific immune response.

prompt.text: Regarding the specificity of the adaptive immune system, how many different types of antigens can a single individual B or T cell recognize?

options: Each cell possesses thousands of different receptors, allowing it to recognize any pathogen it encounters.
Each cell has only one type of antigen-specific receptor, meaning it can only recognize one specific epitope.
B cells can recognize millions of different antigens, while T cells are limited to only three specific types.
The number of antigens a cell can recognize changes every hour based on the body's internal temperature.
questions.answer: Each cell has only one type of antigen-specific receptor, meaning it can only recognize one specific epitope.

prompt.text: What is an 'epitope' in the context of an immune response?

options: The entire DNA sequence of a pathogenic bacterium.
The specific small, accessible portion of an antigen that an antigen receptor actually binds to.
A specialized enzyme used by macrophages to dissolve the cell wall of a virus.
The tail-like structure that allows a B cell to move through the lymphatic system.
questions.answer: The specific small, accessible portion of an antigen that an antigen receptor actually binds to.

prompt.text: Which of the following best describes the difference in how B cells and T cells recognize antigens?

options: B cells only recognize antigens that have been dissolved in gastric juice, while T cells recognize intact proteins on the skin.
B cells bind to intact antigens on the surface of pathogens or in body fluids, while T cells only bind to fragments of antigens presented on the surface of host cells.
B cells must have antigens presented to them by red blood cells, while T cells can bind to any intact virus they encounter in the plasma.
B cells recognize only the internal DNA of a pathogen, while T cells bind to the intact outer lipid membrane of a bacterium.
questions.answer: B cells bind to intact antigens on the surface of pathogens or in body fluids, while T cells only bind to fragments of antigens presented on the surface of host cells.

prompt.text: What are the specialized host cell proteins that bind to antigen fragments and display them on the cell surface for T cells to recognize?

options: Immunoglobulins
MHC (Major Histocompatibility Complex) molecules
Complement proteins
Toll-like receptors
questions.answer: MHC (Major Histocompatibility Complex) molecules

prompt.text: During the maturation of lymphocytes in the bone marrow and thymus, what is the typical fate of a B or T cell that displays receptors specific for the body's own molecules?

options: They are converted into memory cells to prevent future internal inflammation.
They undergo apoptosis or are otherwise inactivated to ensure self-tolerance.
They are released into the lymphatic system to serve as the primary defense against cancerous growth.
They are stored in the spleen and only activated during periods of extreme systemic stress.
questions.answer: They undergo apoptosis or are otherwise inactivated to ensure self-tolerance.

prompt.text: What is the primary difference in how B cells and T cells combat pathogens in the adaptive immune system?

options: B cells directly attack infected body cells, while T cells remain in the bone marrow to produce plasma.
B cells provide humoral immunity by secreting antibodies into body fluids, while T cells provide cell-mediated immunity by interacting directly with infected or abnormal cells.
B cells are part of the innate immune system's barrier defense, while T cells are the primary component of the inflammatory response.
B cells only recognize viral antigens, while T cells are specialized exclusively for detecting fungal infections.
questions.answer: B cells provide humoral immunity by secreting antibodies into body fluids, while T cells provide cell-mediated immunity by interacting directly with infected or abnormal cells.

prompt.text: When an antigen activates a specific B cell, what two types of cell populations are produced through the process of clonal selection?

options: Helper T cells and Cytotoxic T cells
Short-lived plasma cells and long-lived memory cells
Red blood cells and platelets
Macrophages and natural killer cells
questions.answer: Short-lived plasma cells and long-lived memory cells

prompt.text: Once a T cell is activated by an antigen-MHC complex, it undergoes clonal selection to produce which two functional populations of cells?

options: Antibody-secreting plasma cells and cytotoxic granules
Effector T cells (such as helper or cytotoxic) and memory T cells
Mast cells and histamine-releasing basophils
Innate phagocytes and adaptive neutrophils
questions.answer: Effector T cells (such as helper or cytotoxic) and memory T cells

prompt.text: Plasma Cells

categories: Use toxic proteins to kill infected host cells directly
Trigger both humoral and cell-mediated responses by releasing cytokines
Secrete thousands of antibodies into the blood
Provide a rapid, enhanced response upon re-exposure to a pathogen
questions.answer: Secrete thousands of antibodies into the blood

prompt.text: Helper T Cells

categories: Use toxic proteins to kill infected host cells directly
Trigger both humoral and cell-mediated responses by releasing cytokines
Secrete thousands of antibodies into the blood
Provide a rapid, enhanced response upon re-exposure to a pathogen
questions.answer: Trigger both humoral and cell-mediated responses by releasing cytokines

prompt.text: Cytotoxic T Cells

categories: Use toxic proteins to kill infected host cells directly
Trigger both humoral and cell-mediated responses by releasing cytokines
Secrete thousands of antibodies into the blood
Provide a rapid, enhanced response upon re-exposure to a pathogen
questions.answer: Use toxic proteins to kill infected host cells directly

prompt.text: Memory Cells

categories: Use toxic proteins to kill infected host cells directly
Trigger both humoral and cell-mediated responses by releasing cytokines
Secrete thousands of antibodies into the blood
Provide a rapid, enhanced response upon re-exposure to a pathogen
questions.answer: Provide a rapid, enhanced response upon re-exposure to a pathogen

prompt.text: Which of the following describes a primary mechanism by which antibodies physically neutralize a virus?

options: They use digestive enzymes to eat the viral protein coat.
They bind to surface proteins of the virus, preventing it from entering a host cell.
They transport the virus to the stomach to be destroyed by hydrochloric acid.
They penetrate the viral membrane and scramble its genetic code.
questions.answer: They bind to surface proteins of the virus, preventing it from entering a host cell.

prompt.text: What is the primary difference in the timing and magnitude between a primary and secondary immune response?

options: The primary response is faster and stronger because it relies on the innate immune system.
The primary response and secondary response are identical in speed and strength.
The secondary response is faster and of greater magnitude because it relies on a reservoir of memory cells.
The secondary response is slower because the body must create new T cells from scratch.
questions.answer: The secondary response is faster and of greater magnitude because it relies on a reservoir of memory cells.

prompt.text: What is the fundamental difference between active immunity and passive immunity?

options: Active immunity is immediate and temporary, while passive immunity takes weeks to develop but lasts a lifetime.
Active immunity involves the body's own production of antibodies and memory cells, while passive immunity involves receiving pre-made antibodies from another source.
Active immunity only protects against bacteria, while passive immunity is used exclusively for viral infections.
Active immunity occurs only through vaccination, while passive immunity only occurs through natural infection.
questions.answer: Active immunity involves the body's own production of antibodies and memory cells, while passive immunity involves receiving pre-made antibodies from another source.

prompt.text: Which characteristic of antibodies explains why an individual can have immunity to one strain of the flu but still be susceptible to a different strain?

options: Antibodies are universal proteins that can bind to any foreign molecular shape.
Antibodies have highly specific variable regions that must provide a complementary fit to a unique epitope.
Antibodies only recognize the internal genetic material of a virus, which is the same in every strain.
Antibodies are large enough to physically wrap around several different types of viruses simultaneously.
questions.answer: Antibodies have highly specific variable regions that must provide a complementary fit to a unique epitope.

prompt.text: In the adaptive immune system, how do the humoral and cell-mediated responses differ in their targets?

options: The humoral response targets pathogens in blood and lymph using antibodies, while the cell-mediated response targets infected host cells using T cells.
The humoral response uses phagocytes to eat bacteria, while the cell-mediated response uses skin barriers to block entry.
The humoral response targets cells that have already died, while the cell-mediated response prevents viruses from ever touching the body.
The humoral response is only active during the primary infection, while the cell-mediated response only functions during a secondary infection.
questions.answer: The humoral response targets pathogens in blood and lymph using antibodies, while the cell-mediated response targets infected host cells using T cells.
